_comment: Master list of all installable tools. Each tool must have a matching case in the OS scripts.
_howToAdd: 1. Add your tool object here following the schema below. 2. Add install commands in each OS script under scripts/. 3. Submit a PR!

_schema.name: string — the key used in scripts (lowercase, no spaces)
_schema.label: string — human-readable display name shown in the CLI menu
_schema.description: string — one sentence explaining what the tool is and why a developer would want it
_schema.whyUseIt: string — a second sentence explaining the main benefit or use case
_schema.defaultSelected: boolean — true = pre-checked in the interactive menu
_schema.category: string — core | runtime | editor | ide | framework | cli | devops | cloud | database | testing | mobile
_schema.tags: string[] — keywords for future filtering feature

categories.core: Essential tools every developer needs from day one
categories.framework: Language frameworks and SDKs for building applications
categories.cli: Command-line utilities that improve your terminal workflow
categories.devops: Tools for containers, orchestration, and infrastructure
categories.cloud: Cloud provider CLIs for AWS, GCP, and Azure
categories.testing: Testing frameworks and code quality tools
categories.mobile: SDKs and tools for iOS and Android development

tools.name: git
tools.label: Git
tools.description: The world's most widely used distributed version control system — tracks every change to your code across time.
tools.whyUseIt: Without Git you cannot collaborate, roll back mistakes, or contribute to any open-source project on GitHub or GitLab.

tools.name: gh
tools.label: GitHub CLI
tools.description: GitHub's official command-line tool — create PRs, review issues, clone repos, and manage releases without leaving your terminal.
tools.whyUseIt: Eliminates constant browser switching and lets you script GitHub workflows in CI or automation scripts.

tools.name: curl
tools.label: curl
tools.description: A command-line HTTP client that transfers data to and from servers — the swiss army knife for testing APIs and downloading files.
tools.whyUseIt: Used in almost every install script and API test; you will find curl commands in nearly every piece of developer documentation.

tools.name: wget
tools.label: wget
tools.description: A non-interactive network file downloader that supports HTTP, HTTPS, and FTP — great for downloading large files or entire websites.
tools.whyUseIt: Unlike curl, wget can resume interrupted downloads and mirror directory trees, making it ideal for automated file retrieval.

tools.name: nodejs
tools.label: Node.js (LTS)
tools.description: A JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine that lets you run JavaScript server-side — comes bundled with npm, the world's largest package registry.
tools.whyUseIt: Required for any JavaScript or TypeScript project, front-end tooling (Webpack, Vite, ESLint), and back-end APIs built with Express, Fastify, or NestJS.

tools.name: nvm
tools.label: NVM — Node Version Manager
tools.description: Lets you install and switch between multiple versions of Node.js on the same machine without conflicts.
tools.whyUseIt: Essential when working on multiple projects that require different Node versions — run `nvm use 18` to switch instantly.

tools.name: python3
tools.label: Python 3
tools.description: A versatile, beginner-friendly programming language used in web development, data science, machine learning, scripting, and automation.
tools.whyUseIt: Python is the most popular language for AI/ML projects (TensorFlow, PyTorch), data analysis (Pandas, NumPy), and general-purpose scripting.

tools.name: java
tools.label: Java JDK 21 (LTS)
tools.description: Oracle's long-term-support Java Development Kit — provides the compiler, runtime, and standard library for building Java applications.
tools.whyUseIt: Required for Spring Boot, Android development, and any enterprise Java project — JDK 21 is the current LTS release and introduces virtual threads.

tools.name: dotnet
tools.label: .NET SDK
tools.description: Microsoft's free, open-source, cross-platform framework for building web apps, desktop apps, mobile apps, games, and microservices with C# or F#.
tools.whyUseIt: Required for any C# project — includes ASP.NET Core for REST APIs, Blazor for web UIs, and MAUI for cross-platform desktop/mobile apps.

tools.name: go
tools.label: Go (Golang)
tools.description: Google's statically typed, compiled language — famous for its simplicity, fast compilation, and built-in concurrency with goroutines.
tools.whyUseIt: The language behind Docker, Kubernetes, and Terraform — excellent for building high-performance CLI tools, microservices, and cloud infrastructure.

tools.name: rust
tools.label: Rust
tools.description: A systems programming language focused on memory safety, zero-cost abstractions, and fearless concurrency — no garbage collector required.
tools.whyUseIt: Adopted for performance-critical code, WebAssembly, and as a safer replacement for C/C++ — powering tools like ripgrep, fd, and exa.

tools.name: ruby
tools.label: Ruby
tools.description: A dynamic, expressive, object-oriented scripting language famous for its clean syntax and developer happiness.
tools.whyUseIt: The language behind Ruby on Rails — still widely used in startups and enterprise web apps, and required for mobile CI/CD tools like Fastlane.

tools.name: php
tools.label: PHP 8
tools.description: A widely-used server-side scripting language that powers over 75% of the web — the backbone of WordPress, Laravel, and Symfony.
tools.whyUseIt: Essential for WordPress development and building REST APIs with Laravel — PHP 8+ introduced JIT compilation, match expressions, and fibers.

tools.name: vscode
tools.label: Visual Studio Code
tools.description: Microsoft's free, lightweight, enormously popular code editor with built-in Git, IntelliSense auto-complete, a debugger, and a huge extension marketplace.
tools.whyUseIt: The number one editor used by developers worldwide — extensions cover every language, framework, linter, formatter, and remote development scenario imaginable.

tools.name: intellij
tools.label: IntelliJ IDEA Community
tools.description: JetBrains' powerful Java and Kotlin IDE with deep code understanding, advanced refactoring tools, built-in Maven/Gradle support, and integrated debugging.
tools.whyUseIt: The gold-standard IDE for Java and Spring Boot development — its intelligent code inspection catches bugs before you even run the app.

tools.name: pycharm
tools.label: PyCharm Community
tools.description: JetBrains' dedicated Python IDE with intelligent code completion, a built-in debugger, virtual environment management, and Django/Flask support.
tools.whyUseIt: Makes Python development dramatically more productive — automatically finds import errors, manages virtual environments, and integrates pytest out of the box.

tools.name: webstorm
tools.label: WebStorm
tools.description: JetBrains' JavaScript and TypeScript IDE with deep framework support for React, Vue, Angular, and Node.js — includes built-in linting and test runners.
tools.whyUseIt: The most intelligent JS/TS IDE available — understands your entire codebase to provide accurate refactoring and auto-complete across large projects.

tools.name: vim
tools.label: Vim
tools.description: The legendary terminal-based modal text editor — edit files at keyboard speed once you learn its motion commands.
tools.whyUseIt: Available on every Unix system by default — essential for editing files over SSH on remote servers where no GUI is available.

tools.name: neovim
tools.label: Neovim
tools.description: A modernised, community-driven fork of Vim with Lua configuration, a built-in LSP client for code intelligence, async operations, and a rich plugin ecosystem.
tools.whyUseIt: All the power of Vim with modern IDE features — native code completion, inline diagnostics, Treesitter syntax highlighting, and Telescope fuzzy finding.

tools.name: docker
tools.label: Docker
tools.description: A containerisation platform that packages your app and all its dependencies into a portable, isolated container that runs identically on any machine.
tools.whyUseIt: Eliminates 'works on my machine' problems — spin up databases, services, and entire dev environments with a single command and tear them down just as fast.

tools.name: kubectl
tools.label: kubectl — Kubernetes CLI
tools.description: The command-line tool for controlling Kubernetes clusters — deploy apps, inspect pods, view logs, and manage cluster configuration from your terminal.
tools.whyUseIt: The primary interface for any developer working with Kubernetes — required for deploying to EKS, GKE, AKS, or any self-hosted cluster.

tools.name: terraform
tools.label: Terraform
tools.description: HashiCorp's Infrastructure as Code tool — define your entire cloud infrastructure in declarative config files and provision it with one command.
tools.whyUseIt: Replaces clicking through cloud dashboards — version-control, review, and reproduce your infrastructure identically across dev, staging, and production.

tools.name: ansible
tools.label: Ansible
tools.description: An agentless IT automation tool that configures servers, deploys applications, and orchestrates multi-tier workflows using human-readable YAML playbooks.
tools.whyUseIt: Write a playbook once and replay it identically on 1 server or 1000 — no agents to install, just SSH access and Python on the target machine.

tools.name: awscli
tools.label: AWS CLI v2
tools.description: Amazon's official command-line interface for interacting with over 200 AWS services — manage S3, EC2, Lambda, RDS, and more from your terminal.
tools.whyUseIt: Enables scripted AWS workflows, automated deployments, and is required by tools like AWS SAM CLI, CDK, and the Serverless Framework.

tools.name: gcloud
tools.label: Google Cloud CLI
tools.description: Google's official CLI for interacting with GCP services — manage Compute Engine, Cloud Run, GKE, BigQuery, and more from the terminal.
tools.whyUseIt: Required for deploying to Google Cloud and integrating with GCP services like Firebase, Pub/Sub, and Google Kubernetes Engine.

tools.name: azure-cli
tools.label: Azure CLI
tools.description: Microsoft's command-line tool for managing Azure resources — deploy apps, manage VMs, configure networking, and automate Azure workflows.
tools.whyUseIt: Essential for any developer deploying .NET applications, containers, or serverless functions to Microsoft Azure cloud services.

tools.name: mongodb
tools.label: MongoDB Community
tools.description: A leading NoSQL document database that stores data as flexible JSON-like BSON documents — scales horizontally and integrates naturally with JavaScript.
tools.whyUseIt: The 'M' in the MERN stack — pairs naturally with Node.js and Express for full-stack JavaScript apps with dynamic, schema-less data structures.

tools.name: postgresql
tools.label: PostgreSQL
tools.description: A powerful, open-source relational database known for ACID compliance, advanced data types (JSON, arrays, UUID), full-text search, and excellent performance.
tools.whyUseIt: The most feature-rich open-source SQL database — used by Heroku, Supabase, and countless production systems; its JSON support makes it nearly NoSQL-capable too.

tools.name: mysql
tools.label: MySQL
tools.description: The world's most popular open-source relational database — fast, reliable, and the default database for LAMP/MAMP stacks, WordPress, and many legacy apps.
tools.whyUseIt: Required for countless legacy and modern web apps — if you are working with PHP/Laravel, WordPress, or older Rails applications, you will likely need MySQL.

tools.name: redis
tools.label: Redis
tools.description: An in-memory key-value data store used as a cache, message broker, and session store — lightning-fast because all data lives in RAM.
tools.whyUseIt: Dramatically speeds up web apps by caching expensive database queries, and powers features like rate limiting, real-time leaderboards, and job queues (BullMQ).

tools.name: angular-cli
tools.label: Angular CLI (@angular/cli)
tools.description: The official command-line interface for Angular — scaffolds components, services, and modules, and runs the dev server, linter, and test runner with `ng` commands.
tools.whyUseIt: Angular projects are complex to configure by hand — the CLI enforces Angular's opinionated architecture, generates boilerplate instantly, and manages upgrades between major versions.

tools.name: create-react-app
tools.label: Create React App
tools.description: Facebook's official zero-config React scaffolder — sets up Webpack, Babel, ESLint, and Jest automatically so you can start building immediately.
tools.whyUseIt: The fastest way to start a React project without spending hours on build tooling — the 'R' in the MERN stack alongside MongoDB, Express, and Node.js.

tools.name: vite
tools.label: Vite
tools.description: A next-generation front-end build tool that uses native ES modules for near-instant dev server startup and blazing-fast Hot Module Replacement.
tools.whyUseIt: Starts a dev server in milliseconds regardless of project size — supports React, Vue, Svelte, and vanilla JS/TS and is rapidly replacing Create React App.

tools.name: nextjs
tools.label: Next.js (create-next-app)
tools.description: The React framework for production — adds server-side rendering, static site generation, file-based routing, image optimisation, and API routes on top of React.
tools.whyUseIt: The go-to choice for production React apps that need SEO, performance, and a full-stack back-end API in the same codebase — deployed easily on Vercel.

tools.name: express
tools.label: Express.js
tools.description: The minimalist, unopinionated Node.js web framework — provides routing, middleware chaining, and HTTP utilities for building REST APIs and web servers.
tools.whyUseIt: The 'E' in both MERN and MEAN stacks — the most popular way to build Node.js back-ends, with a massive ecosystem of plug-and-play middleware.

tools.name: spring-cli
tools.label: Spring Boot CLI
tools.description: The command-line tool for bootstrapping and running Spring Boot applications — quickly scaffold new projects with chosen dependencies from your terminal.
tools.whyUseIt: Skips the Spring Initializr web UI — generate Spring Boot projects with REST, JPA, Security, and more directly from the terminal in seconds.

tools.name: maven
tools.label: Apache Maven
tools.description: Java's most widely used build automation and dependency management tool — defines project structure and third-party dependencies in a pom.xml file.
tools.whyUseIt: Required for most Java and Spring projects — downloads dependencies from Maven Central, compiles code, runs tests, and packages your app into a JAR or WAR.

tools.name: gradle
tools.label: Gradle
tools.description: A flexible, fast build automation tool for Java, Kotlin, and Android projects — uses a Groovy or Kotlin DSL instead of verbose XML.
tools.whyUseIt: Faster than Maven for large projects thanks to incremental builds and build caching — the default build tool for Android and increasingly preferred for Spring apps.

tools.name: zsh
tools.label: Zsh (Z Shell)
tools.description: An extended Bash-compatible shell with smarter tab completion, spelling correction, glob patterns, and a highly customisable prompt.
tools.whyUseIt: The default shell on macOS since Catalina — pair it with Oh My Zsh and plugins like zsh-autosuggestions to supercharge your terminal productivity.

tools.name: ohmyzsh
tools.label: Oh My Zsh
tools.description: A community-driven framework for managing Zsh configuration — includes 300+ plugins (git, docker, kubectl) and 150+ themes out of the box.
tools.whyUseIt: Installing the git plugin alone — which shows your current branch and dirty status in the prompt — makes it completely worth it; the terminal experience is transformed.

tools.name: tmux
tools.label: tmux — Terminal Multiplexer
tools.description: Splits your terminal into multiple panes, lets you create named windows, and keeps sessions alive so they survive SSH disconnects.
tools.whyUseIt: Run your dev server, test watcher, and git log side-by-side in a single window — and long-running processes stay alive even after closing your laptop lid.

tools.name: htop
tools.label: htop
tools.description: A colourful, interactive terminal process viewer showing CPU, memory, and process trees in real time — a massive upgrade over the plain `top` command.
tools.whyUseIt: Instantly see what is eating your CPU or RAM, sort processes by resource usage, and kill runaway processes — all from the keyboard without leaving the terminal.

tools.name: jq
tools.label: jq — JSON Processor
tools.description: A lightweight, flexible command-line JSON processor — parse, filter, transform, and format JSON from API responses or log files with a simple query syntax.
tools.whyUseIt: Essential for working with REST API responses in shell scripts — pipe curl output into `jq '.users[0].name'` to extract exactly the field you need.

tools.name: make
tools.label: Make
tools.description: A classic Unix build automation tool that reads a Makefile to run named commands — widely used as a simple project task runner.
tools.whyUseIt: A Makefile acts as a self-documenting list of project commands — type `make test` or `make docker-up` instead of memorising long CLI invocations.

tools.name: jest
tools.label: Jest
tools.description: Facebook's all-in-one JavaScript testing framework with a built-in test runner, assertion library, powerful mocking system, and code coverage reports.
tools.whyUseIt: The default testing solution for React, Next.js, and Node.js projects — zero configuration needed and snapshot testing makes UI regression testing trivial.

tools.name: pytest
tools.label: pytest
tools.description: Python's most popular testing framework — discovers and runs tests automatically, supports clean assertions without boilerplate, and extends via 300+ plugins.
tools.whyUseIt: Far less boilerplate than Python's built-in unittest — fixtures, parametrize decorators, and the pytest-cov plugin make it the standard choice for Python projects.

tools.name: postman
tools.label: Postman
tools.description: A GUI API platform for building, testing, documenting, and monitoring REST, GraphQL, and SOAP APIs — with a collection runner and JavaScript test scripting.
tools.whyUseIt: The industry standard for API testing — test endpoints visually, write automated test suites, generate API documentation, and share collections with your team.

tools.name: flutter
tools.label: Flutter SDK
tools.description: Google's open-source UI toolkit for building natively compiled apps for mobile, web, and desktop from a single Dart codebase.
tools.whyUseIt: Write once, deploy to iOS, Android, Web, macOS, Windows, and Linux — Flutter's widget system produces beautiful 60/120fps UIs with no JavaScript bridge overhead.

tools.name: android-studio
tools.label: Android Studio
tools.description: Google's official IDE for Android development — includes the Android SDK, AVD emulator, Layout Editor, APK analyser, and Gradle build system integration.
tools.whyUseIt: The only fully supported IDE for native Android development — the emulator lets you test your app on any screen size and Android version without physical hardware.
